title: A Perturbation Approach to Unconstrained Linear Bandits
abstract: We revisit the standard perturbation-based approach of Abernethy et al. (2008) in the context of unconstrained Bandit Linear Optimization (uBLO). We show the surprising result that in the unconstrained setting, this approach effectively reduces Bandit Linear Optimization (BLO) to a standard Online Linear Optimization (OLO) problem. Our framework improves on prior work in several ways. First, we derive expected-regret guarantees when our perturbation scheme is combined with comparator-adaptive OLO algorithms, leading to new insights about the impact of different adversarial models on the resulting comparator-adaptive rates. We also extend our analysis to dynamic regret, obtaining the optimal $\sqrt{P_T}$ path-length dependencies without prior knowledge of $P_T$. We then develop the first high-probability guarantees for both static and dynamic regret in uBLO. Finally, we discuss lower bounds on the static regret, and prove the folklore $Ω(\sqrt{dT})$ rate for adversarial linear bandits on the unit Euclidean ball, which is of independent interest.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Online convex optimization (OCO) provides a general framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty, in which a learner repeatedly selects an action from a set 𝒲 ⊆ ℝ d \mathcal{W}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{d} and receives feedback generated by an adversarial environment (Shalev-Shwartz, 2012 ; Hazan et al., 2016 ; Orabona, 2019 ) .
The standard measure of performance is regret , which compares the learner’s cumulative loss to that of some unknown benchmark strategy. The most general formulation is dynamic regret , defined by R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) = ∑ t = 1 T f t ​ ( w t ) − ∑ t = 1 T f t ​ ( u t ) , R_{T}(u_{1:T})\;=\;\sum_{t=1}^{T}f_{t}(w_{t})\;-\;\sum_{t=1}^{T}f_{t}(u_{t}), where f t : 𝒲 → ℝ f_{t}:\mathcal{W}\to\mathbb{R} denotes the convex loss function on round t t , each play w t ∈ 𝒲 w_{t}\in\mathcal{W} of the learner is based solely on its past observations, and u 1 : T = ( u t ) t ∈ [ T ] u_{1:T}=(u_{t})_{t\in[T]} is a comparator sequence in 𝒲 \mathcal{W} . The classical static regret is recovered as the special case u 1 = ⋯ = u T u_{1}=\cdots=u_{T} .

In this paper we focus on Bandit Linear Optimization (BLO) Flaxman et al. ( 2005 ); Kalai & Vempala ( 2005 ); Dani et al. ( 2007 ); Lattimore & Szepesvári ( 2020 ); Lattimore ( 2024 ) , where f t f_{t} is a linear function and the learner observes only the scalar output f t ​ ( w t ) = ⟨ ℓ t , w t ⟩ f_{t}(w_{t})=\left\langle\ell_{t},w_{t}\right\rangle for some vector ℓ t ∈ ℝ d \ell_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d} , rather than the full gradient ∇ f t ​ ( w t ) = ℓ t \nabla f_{t}(w_{t})=\ell_{t} . We propose a modular reduction that enables the use of an arbitrary OLO learner under bandit feedback by feeding it suitably perturbed loss estimates. We focus in particular on unconstrained bandit linear optimization (uBLO), in which the action set is 𝒲 = ℝ d \mathcal{W}=\mathbb{R}^{d} (van der Hoeven et al., 2020 ; Luo et al., 2022 ; Rumi et al., 2026 ) .
A central objective in uBLO is to obtain comparator-adaptive guarantees, in which the regret against a static comparator u u scales with ‖ u ‖ \|u\| , while simultaneously enforcing a risk-control constraint of the form R T ​ ( 0 ) ≤ ϵ R_{T}(0)\leq\epsilon , where ϵ \epsilon is a fixed, user-specified parameter.

This formulation is closely connected to parameter-free online learning and coin-betting (Mcmahan & Streeter, 2012 ; Orabona & Pál, 2016 ; Cutkosky & Orabona, 2018 ) : controlling R T ​ ( 0 ) R_{T}(0) can be interpreted as allowing the bettor to reinvest a data-dependent fraction of an initial budget and its accumulated gains, rather than committing to a fixed betting scale (Orabona, 2019 , Chapter 5.3) . This viewpoint highlights how uBLO complements the more standard linear bandit setting with a bounded action set. In the bounded case, the learner effectively plays with a fixed “unit budget” each round (since ‖ w t ‖ \|w_{t}\| is uniformly bounded), whereas in the unconstrained case the learner may increase its effective scale over time, but only if its total gains permit it. In applications where exploration must be performed under budget constraints, this built-in risk control can make the unconstrained model (perhaps counterintuitively) the more natural abstraction.

In this work we also broaden the standard notion of comparator-adaptivity by allowing the adversary to choose the comparator norm more strategically, e.g., at the end of the interaction, refining what “adaptive” guarantees entail.

sections.heading: 1.1Related Works
sections.text:
Bandit Linear Optimization (BLO), also known as adversarial linear bandits ,
has a long history McMahan & Blum ( 2004 ); Awerbuch & Kleinberg ( 2004 ); Dani & Hayes ( 2006 ); Dani et al. ( 2007 ); Abernethy et al. ( 2008 ); Bubeck et al. ( 2012 ) . In these works, the action set 𝒲 \mathcal{W} is typically constrained to a bounded set, and minimax-optimal guarantees on the expected regret are known to be of order d ​ T d\sqrt{T} or d ​ T \sqrt{dT} depending on the geometry of the decision set (Dani et al., 2007 ; Shamir, 2015 ) . More recently, these results have also been extended to nearly-matching high-probability guarantees Lee et al. ( 2020 ); Zimmert & Lattimore ( 2022 ) .

Our work is most closely related to
the works of van der Hoeven et al. ( 2020 ); Luo et al. ( 2022 ); Rumi et al. ( 2026 ) , which investigate linear bandit problems with unconstrained action sets (uBLO). van der Hoeven et al. ( 2020 ) provided the first approach for this setting, using a variant of the scale/direction decomposition from the parameter-free online learning literature (Cutkosky & Orabona, 2018 ) . As remarked by Luo et al. ( 2022 ) , using their approach with a direction learner admitting 𝒪 ~ ​ ( d ​ T ) \widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{dT}) regret on the unit ball (Bubeck et al., 2012 ) , one can obtain a 𝒪 ~ ​ ( ‖ u ‖ ​ ( d ∨ log ⁡ ( ‖ u ‖ ) ) ​ T ) \widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\|u\|\sqrt{(d\vee\log(\|u\|))T}) static regret bound. Our work provides new insights into these results by highlighting a subtle dependency issue between the loss sequence and the comparator norm, addressed by our approach.

Later, Rumi et al. ( 2026 ) investigated dynamic regret in the uBLO setting, and achieved the first guarantees in the (oblivious) adversarial setting that adapt to the number of switches of the comparator sequence, S T = ∑ t 𝕀 ​ { u t ≠ u t − 1 } S_{T}=\sum_{t}\mathbb{I}\left\{u_{t}\neq u_{t-1}\right\} , while guaranteeing a S T \sqrt{S_{T}} dependence without prior knowledge of the comparator sequence. Besides this work, all existing works on dynamic regret under bandit feedback fail to obtain the optimal S T \sqrt{S_{T}} dependencies without leveraging prior knowledge
of the comparator sequence (Agarwal et al., 2017 ; Marinov & Zimmert, 2021 ; Luo et al., 2022 ) , and in fact Marinov & Zimmert ( 2021 ) show that S T \sqrt{S_{T}} dependencies are impossible against an adaptive adversary in many constrained settings. 1 1 1 In particular, their lower bound
holds for finite policy classes.

Our work is also related to the recent line of work in online convex optimization on comparator-adaptive (sometimes called parameter-free ) methods.
These are algorithms which, for any fixed ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 , achieve
guarantees of the form R T ​ ( u ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( ϵ + ‖ u ‖ ​ T ​ log ⁡ ( ‖ u ‖ ​ T ϵ + 1 ) ) , \displaystyle R_{T}(u)=\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\left(\epsilon+\|u\|\sqrt{T\log\left(\tfrac{\|u\|\sqrt{T}}{\epsilon}+1\right)}\right), (1) uniformly over all u ∈ ℝ d u\in\mathbb{R}^{d} simultaneously, matching the bound that gradient descent would obtain with oracle tuning (up to logarithmic factors) (Mcmahan & Streeter, 2012 ; McMahan & Orabona, 2014 ; Orabona & Pál, 2016 ; Cutkosky & Orabona, 2018 ; Orabona & Pál, 2021 ; Mhammedi & Koolen, 2020 ; Zhang & Cutkosky, 2022 ) . The key feature of Equation ˜ 1 is that the bound is adaptive to an arbitrary comparator norm, rather than
the worst-case D = sup x , y ∈ 𝒲 ‖ x − y ‖ D=\sup_{x,y\in\mathcal{W}}\|x-y\| ,
making these methods crucial for unconstrained settings. Comparator-adaptive methods have also recently been extended to dynamic regret Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ); Zhang et al. ( 2023 ); Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2023 ); Jacobsen & Orabona ( 2024 ); Jacobsen et al. ( 2025 ) , with guarantees
that adapt to the path-length P T ≔ ∑ t = 2 T ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ P_{T}\coloneqq\sum_{t=2}^{T}\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\| and effective diameter M = max t ⁡ ‖ u t ‖ M=\max_{t}\|u_{t}\| in unbounded domains R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) ≤ O ~ ​ ( ( M 2 + M ​ P T ) ​ T ) . \displaystyle R_{T}(u_{1:T})\leq\widetilde{O}\left(\sqrt{(M^{2}+MP_{T})T}\right)~. (2) In this work we use a less common generalization of
this bound due to Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) , which adapts to the path-length and each of the individual comparator norms to achieve R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) ≤ O ~ ​ ( ( ‖ u T ‖ + P T ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ ) . \displaystyle R_{T}(u_{1:T})\leq\widetilde{O}\Big(\sqrt{(\|u_{T}\|+P_{T}){\textstyle\sum_{t=1}^{T}}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}\|u_{t}\|}\Big). (3) Our approach to achieves the first bounds of form in the bandit setting.

sections.heading: 1.2Contributions
sections.text:
In this paper we revisit the classic perturbation-based approach of Abernethy et al. ( 2008 ) in the setting on unconstrained linear bandits (Section 2 ), under the name PABLO ( Perturbation Approach for Bandit Linear Optimization ).
We show that this approach produces loss estimators with strong properties, enabling us to develop several novel results in the uBLO setting.

sections.heading: 2Perturbation-based approach
sections.text:
In this section we introduce a simple reduction that turns any algorithm for Online Linear Optimization (OLO) into an algorithm for Bandit Linear Optimization (BLO), inspired by the SCRiBLe algorithm of Abernethy et al. ( 2008 , 2012 ) . We will refer to this approach as PABLO , short for a Perturbed Approach to Bandit Linear Optimization . The pseudo-code of PABLO can be found in Algorithm 1 .
On each round t t , the algorithm operates in two steps. First, an OLO learner 𝒜 \mathcal{A} outputs a decision w t ∈ 𝒲 w_{t}\in\mathcal{W} based on past feedback. Then the algorithm applies a randomized perturbation w ~ t \widetilde{w}_{t} of w t w_{t} , observes the bandit feedback, and constructs an unbiased estimator ℓ ~ t \widetilde{\ell}_{t} of the loss ℓ t \ell_{t} , which is then passed back to 𝒜 \mathcal{A} .

This is a modest generalization of SCRiBLe, decoupling the OLO update from the perturbation mechanism. In the original SCRiBLe algorithm, both components are tied to a single self-concordant barrier ψ \psi : the OLO step is implemented via FTRL with regularizer ψ \psi , and the perturbation is scaled using the local geometry induced by ∇ 2 ψ ​ ( w t ) \nabla^{2}\psi(w_{t}) , which is replaced by a matrix H t 1 2 H_{t}^{\frac{1}{2}} in PABLO . As we show in Section 3 , this coupling is not necessary in several instances of unconstrained Bandit Linear Optimization, where it can be advantageous to tune the OLO algorithm and the perturbation level separately.

sections.heading: 2.1Generic Expected Regret Analysis for BLO
sections.text:
We now state a generic reduction showing how the expected-regret guarantees of PABLO follow from those of the underlying OLO routine 𝒜 \mathcal{A} .

The proof is deferred to Appendix ˜ A , and is based on a standard ghost-iterate trick (see, e.g., Nemirovski et al. ( 2009 ); Neu & Okolo ( 2024 ) ). More generally, the same reduction applies to any bandit algorithm that plays a randomized action whose conditional expectation equals the iterate produced by an OLO routine. Our perturbation scheme is one concrete instantiation of this principle, and has the particular advantage of having loss estimates that are both unbiased and bounded almost-surely, enabling us to apply comparator-adaptive OLO algorithms which require a bound on the gradient norms.

sections.heading: 3Novel expected regret bounds for uBLO
sections.text:
In this section we present several applications of Proposition 2.1 , leading to new algorithms and regret guarantees for the uBLO setting within the PABLO framework. A key feature of uBLO is that the action domain is unbounded, so the perturbation matrices ( H t ) t ≥ 1 (H_{t})_{t\geq 1} are not constrained by feasibility considerations and only need to satisfy the condition of the proposition. In the remainder of the paper, we therefore adopt the simple isotropic choice ∀ t ≥ 1 , H t = 1 d ​ ( ‖ w t ‖ 2 ∨ ε 2 ) ​ I d , \forall t\geq 1,\quad H_{t}\;=\;\frac{1}{d(\|w_{t}\|^{2}\vee\varepsilon^{2})}\,I_{d}, (4) for ε ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \varepsilon\in(0,1) ,
and focus on how different regret guarantees arise from different choices of the underlying OLO routine.

sections.heading: 3.1Static Regret via Parameter-free OLO
sections.text:
We first instantiate PABLO for uBLO by choosing, as OLO an subroutine, the parameter-free mirror descent (PFMD) algorithm of Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 , Section 3) . This choice is motivated by its strong static comparator-adaptive guarantee on the unconstrained domain: for any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 and any sequence ( g t ) t ≥ 1 (g_{t})_{t\geq 1} , its regret satisfies R T 𝒜 ​ ( u ) = O ~ ​ ( G ​ ϵ + ‖ u ‖ ​ V T ​ log + ⁡ ( ‖ u ‖ ​ V T G ​ ϵ ) ) , R_{T}^{\mathcal{A}}(u)=\widetilde{O}\!\left(G\epsilon+\|u\|\,\sqrt{V_{T}\,\log_{+}\left(\frac{\|u\|\sqrt{V_{T}}}{G\epsilon}\right)}\right), where V T = ∑ t = 1 T ‖ g t ‖ 2 2 V_{T}=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|g_{t}\|^{2}_{2} ,
uniformly over u ∈ ℝ d u\in\mathbb{R}^{d} .
To turn ( 3.1 ) into a bandit guarantee for PABLO ​, we then apply Proposition 2.1 and obtain the following result.

Proof of Theorem ˜ 3.1 can be found in Appendix ˜ B . A key subtlety, and the reason Theorem ˜ 3.1 yields two distinct guarantees, is that using Jensen’s inequality to obtain an upper bound of the form 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ u ‖ ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 2 ] ≤ ‖ u ‖ ​ ∑ t = 1 T 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 2 ] \mathbb{E}\!\left[\|u\|\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|_{2}^{2}}\right]\;\leq\;\|u\|\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{T}\mathbb{E}\left[\|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|_{2}^{2}\right]} is only justified when the scale ‖ u ‖ \|u\| is conditionally independent of the randomness generating ℓ ~ t \widetilde{\ell}_{t} (and thus does not depend on the realized trajectory through the losses and actions). When such independence holds (e.g., ‖ u ‖ \|u\| is chosen obliviously at the start of the game), we can exploit the sharper in-expectation control of ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 2 \|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|_{2}^{2} . Otherwise, for norm-adaptive comparators, we must rely on the more conservative almost-sure bound from Proposition 2 to bound ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 = 𝒪 ​ ( d 2 ​ ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ) \|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|^{2}=\mathcal{O}(d^{2}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}) .

Note that this is generally not a concern in constrained settings: in the standard bounded domain setting, the worst-case comparator is typically on the convex hull of 𝒲 \mathcal{W} , and its norm can be bounded by the diameter of 𝒲 \mathcal{W} .
However, in an unconstrained linear setting, no such finite worst-case comparator exists, and the goal becomes to ensure R T ​ ( u ) ≤ B T ​ ( u ) R_{T}(u)\leq B_{T}(u) for all u ∈ ℝ d u\in\mathbb{R}^{d} simultaneously , where B T ​ ( u ) B_{T}(u) is some non-negative function.
This makes the natural worst-case comparator
have a data-dependent norm, e.g., ‖ u ‖ ∝ exp ⁡ ( ‖ ∑ t = 1 T ℓ t ‖ 2 G 2 ​ T ) \|u\|\propto\exp\left(\frac{\|\sum_{t=1}^{T}\ell_{t}\|^{2}}{G^{2}T}\right) when choosing B T ​ ( u ) B_{T}(u) to match the minimax optimal bound for OLO
(see Appendix H for details). Because of this, from a comparator-adaptive perspective, it is not natural to treat the comparator norm as independent of the losses or the learner’s decisions without
additional explicit assumptions.

Interestingly the above observation does not seem to be accounted for in many prior works. Indeed, as far as we are aware all prior works in this setting are implicitly making the assumption that the comparator norm is
oblivious rather than adaptive (van der Hoeven et al., 2020 ; Luo et al., 2022 ; Rumi et al., 2026 ) , and the stated guarantees can potentially be very different without this assumption,
as detailed in the following discussion.

sections.heading: 3.2Dynamic Regret in Expectation
sections.text:
To achieve dynamic regret guarantees, we now apply PABLO with a suitable OLO algorithm for unconstrained dynamic regret. The following
results shows that the optimal P T \sqrt{P_{T}} dependence can be obtained
by leveraging the parameter-free dynamic regret algorithm
of Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) . In fact, we provide a modest refinement of their result which removes M = max t ⁡ ‖ u t ‖ M=\max_{t}\|u_{t}\| completely from the main term in the bound, and
showcases a refined measure of comparator variability: the linearithmic path-length P T Φ = ∑ t = 2 T ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ ​ log ⁡ ( ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ ​ T 3 / ϵ + 1 ) \displaystyle P_{T}^{\Phi}=\sum_{t=2}^{T}\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\|\log\left(\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\|T^{3}/\epsilon+1\right) which is an adaptive refinement of the P T ​ log ⁡ ( M ​ T 3 / ϵ + 1 ) P_{T}\log\left(MT^{3}/\epsilon+1\right) dependence reported by Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) . This result is of independent interest
and provided in Appendix ˜ E . Then applying PABLO with this dynamic regret algorithm leads to the following
expected dynamic regret guarantee for uBLO.

Theorem 3.3 . For any sequence u 1 : T = ( u t ) t = 1 T u_{1:T}=(u_{t})_{t=1}^{T} in ℝ d \mathbb{R}^{d} , PABLO equipped with Algorithm ˜ 6 tuned with ϵ / d \epsilon/d guarantees 𝔼 ​ [ R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) ] \displaystyle\mathbb{E}\left[R_{T}(u_{1:T})\right] = 𝒪 ​ ( 𝔼 ​ [ d κ ​ ( Φ T + P T Φ ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ + d ​ G ​ ( ϵ + max t ⁡ ‖ u t ‖ + Φ T + P T Φ ) ] ) . \displaystyle=\mathcal{O}\Bigg(\mathbb{E}\Bigg[\frac{d}{\kappa}\sqrt{(\Phi_{T}+P_{T}^{\Phi})\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}\|u_{t}\|}+dG(\epsilon+\max_{t}\|u_{t}\|+\Phi_{T}+P_{T}^{\Phi})\Bigg]\Bigg). where κ = d \kappa=\sqrt{d} in the oblivious setting
and κ = 1 \kappa=1 otherwise,
and we define Φ T = ‖ u T ‖ ​ log ⁡ ( ‖ u T ‖ ​ T ϵ + 1 ) \Phi_{T}=\|u_{T}\|\log\left(\tfrac{\|u_{T}\|T}{\epsilon}+1\right) and P T Φ = ∑ t = 2 T ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ ​ log ⁡ ( ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ ​ T 3 ϵ + 1 ) P_{T}^{\Phi}=\sum_{t=2}^{T}\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\|\log\left(\tfrac{\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\|T^{3}}{\epsilon}+1\right) .

Proof of the theorem can be found in Appendix ˜ C .
The closest result to ours is the recent work of Rumi et al. ( 2026 ) , which also achieves adaptivity to the
comparator sequence without prior knowledge. However, their result scales with the switching number S T = ∑ t = 2 T 𝕀 ​ { u t ≠ u t − 1 } S_{T}=\sum_{t=2}^{T}\mathbb{I}\left\{u_{t}\neq u_{t-1}\right\} ,
which is closely related to P T P_{T} but is a weaker measure of variation, failing to account for the potentially heterogeneous magnitudes of the increments ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ \|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\| .
Our result is therefore the first to achieve P T \sqrt{P_{T}} adaptivity to the genuine path-length P T P_{T} .
Moreover, their result is restricted to the oblivious adversarial setting, whereas our results remain meaningful
even against fully-adaptive comparator and loss sequences.

Besides achieving the optimal P T \sqrt{P_{T}} dependence, we inherit another novelty from the algorithm of Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) :
the bound of Theorem ˜ 3.3 is also adaptive to the individual comparator norms, with a variance penalty
scaling with ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ \sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}\|u_{t}\| .
This leads to
a property which is similar in spirit to a strongly-adaptive guarantee (Daniely et al., 2015 ) , in the sense that
if the comparator sequence is only active (non-zero) within a sub-interval [ a , b ] [a,b] , then
the regret automatically restricts to that same sub-interval, R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( P [ a , b ] ​ | b − a | ) R_{T}(u_{1:T})=\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{P_{[a,b]}\left|b-a\right|}) , where P [ a , b ] = ∑ t = a + 1 b ‖ u t − u t − 1 ‖ P_{[a,b]}=\sum_{t=a+1}^{b}\|u_{t}-u_{t-1}\| is the path-length over the interval.
While Jacobsen & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) show that one cannot obtain comparator-adaptive guarantees on all sub-intervals simultaneously (thereby extending the impossibility result of Daniely et al. ( 2015 ) to unbounded domains), the per-comparator adaptivity in Theorem ˜ 3.3 can be viewed as a natural, achievable analogue of strong adaptivity in the unbounded setting.

Finally, we again observe a d \sqrt{d} discrepancy between the upper bounds obtained against a norm-oblivious or norm-adaptive adversary, leading to similar insights as observed in previous section.
Likewise, our result more generally holds for the partially-oblivious setting wherein u 1 : T u_{1:T} is ℱ 0 \mathcal{F}_{0} measurable but ℓ 1 : T \ell_{1:T} may be adaptive, in which case
the ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 \|\ell_{t}\|^{2} dependencies are replaced by the more general 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 | ℱ 0 ] \mathbb{E}\left[\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}|\mathcal{F}_{0}\right] , as discussed in Section ˜ 3.1 .

sections.heading: 4High-probability Bounds
sections.text:
In this section
we derive novel
high-probability bounds for both static and dynamic regret, for new instances of PABLO .
As in previous setting,
we begin with a general reduction to unconstrained OLO, and study the additional
penalties that emerge due to the loss estimates. We again use H t H_{t} from
Eq. ( 4 ) in all applications presented in this section, though in
this section we will choose ε 2 = ϵ 2 / T \varepsilon^{2}=\epsilon^{2}/T for any ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 ; this
will ensure that the perturbations H t − 1 2 ​ s t H_{t}^{-\frac{1}{2}}s_{t} in Algorithm ˜ 1 have sufficiently nice concentration
properties in the following reduction.

Proof of the theorem can be found in Section ˜ D.1 . It shows that uBLO can also be effectively
reduced to uOCO (with regard to high probability bounds) at the expense of three
additional terms. The latter two comparator-dependent
terms are fairly benign and amount to a lower-order 𝒪 ​ ( G ​ P T ) \mathcal{O}(GP_{T}) and a 𝒪 ~ ​ ( M ​ d ​ T ​ log ⁡ ( T / δ ) ) \widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(M\sqrt{dT\log\left(T/\delta\right)}) ,
both of which are expected in this setting.
However,
the term G ​ d ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ w t ‖ 2 ​ log ⁡ ( 1 / δ ) G\sqrt{d\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|w_{t}\|^{2}\log\left(1/\delta\right)} is algorithm dependent and
could be arbitrarily large in an unbounded domain.
Thus, the main difficulty to achieve high-probability bounds for PABLO stems from controlling the stability of the iterates w t w_{t} from the OLO learner 𝒜 \mathcal{A} .

Fortunately, there is already an algorithm for
unbounded domains
due to Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) which handles exactly
this issue. Their approach is based on a clever
combination of composite regularization and implicit optimistic updates,
as detailed in Section ˜ D.2 .
Plugging this approach into our framework, and composing Section ˜ 4 with Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 , Theorem 3) , we obtain the
following high-probability guarantee.
Notably, the result matches the best-known
results from the constrained setting (Zimmert & Lattimore, 2022 ) up to the usual 𝒪 ​ ( log ⁡ ( ‖ u ‖ ) ) \mathcal{O}(\log(\|u\|)) penalties
associated with unbounded domains.

Interestingly, a similar strategy leveraging composite regularization and optimism can also be used to obtain
high-probability dynamic regret bounds.
In Section ˜ D.3.2 ( Theorem ˜ D.5 ), we provide
an algorithm 𝒜 η \mathcal{A}_{\eta} that, for any composite penalty φ t : ℝ d → ℝ ≥ 0 \varphi_{t}:\mathbb{R}^{d}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} with ‖ ∇ φ t ​ ( w t ) ‖ ≤ H \|\nabla\varphi_{t}(w_{t})\|\leq H and η ≤ 1 / ( d ​ ( G + H ) ) \eta\leq 1/(d(G+H)) , guarantees
that for any sequences u 1 : T u_{1:T} and ℓ ~ 1 : T \widetilde{\ell}_{1:T} in ℝ d \mathbb{R}^{d} , R ~ T 𝒜 η ​ ( u 1 : T ) ≔ ∑ t = 1 T ⟨ ℓ ~ t , w t η − u t ⟩ \displaystyle\widetilde{R}_{T}^{\mathcal{A}_{\eta}}(u_{1:T})\coloneqq\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left\langle\widetilde{\ell}_{t},w_{t}^{\eta}-u_{t}\right\rangle ≤ 𝒪 ~ ​ ( ‖ u T ‖ + P T η + η ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ + ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u t ) − φ t ​ ( w t η ) ) , \displaystyle\leq\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\Big(\frac{\|u_{T}\|+P_{T}}{\eta}+\eta\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|^{2}\|u_{t}\|+\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u_{t})-\varphi_{t}(w_{t}^{\eta})\Big), which matches the regret bound obtained in the expected dynamic regret setting when η \eta is optimally tuned, but additionally exhibits a term ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u t ) − φ t ​ ( w t η ) \sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u_{t})-\varphi_{t}(w_{t}^{\eta}) . We then obtain the optimal trade-off in η \eta using a standard technique for combining comparator-adaptive guarantees: by running the algorithm in parallel over a grid of values of η \eta and playing w t = ∑ η w t η w_{t}=\sum_{\eta}w_{t}^{\eta} , we obtain the tuned bound R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) = ∑ t = 1 T ⟨ ℓ ~ t , w t − u t ⟩ ≤ 𝒪 ~ ​ ( d ​ ( ‖ u T ‖ + P T ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ + ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u t ) − ∑ η ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( w t η ) ) . R_{T}(u_{1:T})=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left\langle\widetilde{\ell}_{t},w_{t}-u_{t}\right\rangle\leq\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\!\left(d\sqrt{(\|u_{T}\|+P_{T})\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}\,\|u_{t}\|}+\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u_{t})-\sum_{\eta}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(w_{t}^{\eta})\right). Finally, we show that for a carefully-chosen composite penalty φ t \varphi_{t} , the aggregate-iterate ‖ w t ‖ = ‖ ∑ η w t η ‖ \|w_{t}\|=\|\sum_{\eta}w_{t}^{\eta}\| dependencies from Section ˜ 4 are canceled out by the aggregate penalty − ∑ η ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( w t η ) -\sum_{\eta}\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(w_{t}^{\eta}) , while also ensuring that ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u t ) = O ~ ​ ( d ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ u t ‖ 2 ​ log ⁡ ( T / δ ) ) \sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u_{t})=\widetilde{O}\Big(\sqrt{d\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|u_{t}\|^{2}\log\left(T/\delta\right)}\Big) .
The result is the following theorem,
proved in Section ˜ D.3.3 .

The full proof can be found in Sections ˜ D.3 and D.3.3 .
To understand the bound, consider first the branch where Ω T ≤ d ​ 𝒱 T \Omega_{T}\leq d\mathcal{V}_{T} .
In this case the bound reduces to R T \displaystyle R_{T} ( u 1 : T ) ≤ 𝒪 ~ ​ ( d ​ G ​ ( ϵ + M + P T ) ​ log ⁡ ( T δ ) + G ​ M ​ d ​ T ​ log ⁡ ( T δ ) + d ​ ( M 2 + M ​ P T ) ​ T ) . \displaystyle(u_{1:T})\leq\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}\Big(dG(\epsilon+M+P_{T})\log\left(\tfrac{T}{\delta}\right)+GM\sqrt{dT\log\left(\tfrac{T}{\delta}\right)}+\sqrt{d(M^{2}+MP_{T})T}\Big). Therefore, the bound captures the same worst-case G ​ ‖ u ‖ ​ d ​ T G\|u\|\sqrt{dT} bound as Theorem ˜ 4.2 in the
static regret ( P T = 0 P_{T}=0 ) setting,
matching the best-known result from the constrained setting up to poly-logarithmic terms as a special case (Zimmert & Lattimore, 2022 ) .
At the same time, considering the other branch, in which d ​ 𝒱 T ≤ Ω T d\mathcal{V}_{T}\leq\Omega_{T} , we also achieve a per-comparator
adaptivity similar to the result from the
expected regret guarantee in Theorem ˜ 3.1 , with R T ​ ( u 1 : T ) R_{T}(u_{1:T}) bounded by O ~ ​ ( G ​ d ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ u t ‖ 2 ​ log ⁡ ( T δ ) + d ​ ( Φ T + P T Φ ) ​ ∑ t = 1 T ‖ ℓ t ‖ 2 ​ ‖ u t ‖ + d ​ G ​ ( ϵ + M + P T ) ​ log ⁡ ( T δ ) ) . \displaystyle\widetilde{O}\!\Bigg(G\sqrt{\!d\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|u_{t}\|^{2}\log\left(\tfrac{T}{\delta}\right)}+d\sqrt{(\Phi_{T}+P_{T}^{\Phi})\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\ell_{t}\|^{2}\|u_{t}\|}+dG(\epsilon+M+P_{T})\log\left(\tfrac{T}{\delta}\right)\Bigg). Hence the bound retains the
strong-adaptivity-like property discussed in Section ˜ 3.2 , in which the bound automatically restricts to a sub-interval [ a , b ] [a,b] when comparing against comparator sequences
which are only active on [ a , b ] [a,b] , and also avoids M = max t ⁡ ‖ u t ‖ M=\max_{t}\|u_{t}\| in all but lower-order terms in the bound.

sections.heading: 5Towards lower bounds for uBLO
sections.text:
This section provides some insights on lower bounds for unconstrained adversarial linear bandits. We present a conjecture for the static-regret minimax rate, guided by known OCO lower bounds and by a self-contained proof of the folklore Θ ~ ​ ( d ​ T ) \widetilde{\Theta}(\sqrt{dT}) minimax bound on the unit Euclidean ball, which captures the intrinsic difficulty of identifying a favorable direction under mildly biased losses. We then discuss post-hoc comparator norm adaptivity, motivating lower-bound formulations that simultaneously control both the expected comparator norm and its worst-case magnitude.

We recall the scale/direction regret decomposition from van der Hoeven et al. ( 2020 , Lemma 1) . Although none of our algorithms use this approach, it is central for the discussions in this section: at each step t t we decompose the learner’s action x t x_{t} as x t = v t ​ z t x_{t}=v_{t}z_{t} , where v t ∈ ℝ v_{t}\in\mathbb{R} is a scalar (“scale”) and z t ∈ 𝔹 d z_{t}\in\mathbb{B}_{d} is a unit vector (“direction”). Then,
it can be shown (Cutkosky & Orabona, 2018 ) that R T ​ ( u ) = R T 𝒱 ​ ( ‖ u ‖ ) + ‖ u ‖ ​ R T 𝒵 ​ ( u ‖ u ‖ ) , with R_{T}(u)=R_{T}^{\mathcal{V}}(\|u\|)+\|u\|\,R_{T}^{\mathcal{Z}}\!\left(\frac{u}{\|u\|}\right),\text{with} (6) R T 𝒱 ​ ( ‖ u ‖ ) ≔ ∑ t = 1 T ( v t − ‖ u ‖ ) ​ ⟨ z t , ℓ t ⟩ R_{T}^{\mathcal{V}}(\|u\|)\coloneqq\sum_{t=1}^{T}\bigl(v_{t}-\|u\|\bigr)\,\langle z_{t},\ell_{t}\rangle ( scale regret ), and R T 𝒵 ​ ( u ‖ u ‖ ) ≔ ∑ t = 1 T ⟨ z t − u ‖ u ‖ , ℓ t ⟩ R_{T}^{\mathcal{Z}}\!\left(\frac{u}{\|u\|}\right)\coloneqq\sum_{t=1}^{T}\left\langle z_{t}-\frac{u}{\|u\|},\,\ell_{t}\right\rangle ( direction regret ).

sections.heading: 6Future Directions
sections.text:
We leave open several directions for future work.
As discussed in Section ˜ 5 , novel techniques seem necessary to prove complete lower bounds for unconstrained BLO, both against norm-oblivious and norm-adaptive adversaries. It also remains to understand whether a dimension-dependent gap between the two settings can be avoided.

Our dynamic-regret guarantees also raise a natural question: under what conditions can one obtain non-trivial dynamic regret bounds in bandit settings without prior knowledge of, e.g., P T P_{T} ? In many constrained problems, such guarantees are known to be impossible against adaptive adversaries; see for instance, Marinov & Zimmert ( 2021 ) . The uBLO setting may be an extreme regime that evades these lower bounds by removing the domain constraints. An interesting direction is to identify more general assumptions under which P T \sqrt{P_{T}} -type dependencies remain achievable.

Finally, follow-up work could seek to extend our approach to the
more general Bandit Convex Optimization setting, in which
the losses are arbitrary convex functions.

sections.heading: D.1Reduction to OLO
sections.text:
In this section, we prove Section ˜ 4 , which shows that with high-probability, the regret of PABLO scales with the regret of the OLO algorithm 𝒜 \mathcal{A} deployed against the loss estimates ℓ ~ t \widetilde{\ell}_{t} , plus some additional stability terms.

Note that in the following theorem,
the lower-order G ​ ω ​ log ⁡ ( 16 / δ ) G\omega\sqrt{\log\left(16/\delta\right)} can be replaced by a δ \delta -independent G ​ ϵ G\epsilon by setting ω = ϵ / log ⁡ ( 16 / δ ) \omega=\epsilon/\sqrt{\log\left(16/\delta\right)} , and the resulting trade-off can be
bound in terms of log ⁡ ( 1 / δ ) \log\left(1/\delta\right) since the remaining ω \omega -dependent terms are doubly-logarithmic in 1 / ω 1/\omega and can be naively bound as log ⁡ ( 4 δ ​ log ⁡ C ω ) = 𝒪 ​ ( log ⁡ ( 4 ​ log ⁡ ( C / ϵ ) + 2 ​ log ⁡ ( 1 / δ ) δ ) ) = 𝒪 ​ ( log ⁡ ( log ⁡ ( C / ϵ ) δ ) ) \log\left(\frac{4}{\delta}\log\tfrac{C}{\omega}\right)=\mathcal{O}\left(\log\left(\tfrac{4\log(C/\epsilon)+2\log\left(1/\delta\right)}{\delta}\right)\right)=\mathcal{O}\left(\log\left(\tfrac{\log\left(C/\epsilon\right)}{\delta}\right)\right) using log ⁡ ( 1 / δ ) ≤ 1 δ \log\left(1/\delta\right)\leq\frac{1}{\delta} for δ > 0 \delta>0 .
Hence, in the main text we drop the
lower-order dependence on G ​ ω ​ log ⁡ ( 16 / δ ) G\omega\sqrt{\log\left(16/\delta\right)} but leave ω > 0 \omega>0 free here for generality.

sections.heading: D.2High-probability Static Regret Guarantees
sections.text:
In this section we briefly review the approach developed
by Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) for developing high-probability guarantees
in unconstrained settings.

In unconstrained settings, the
usual Martingale concentration arguments alone are not enough to control the bias terms ∑ t = 1 T ⟨ ℓ t − ℓ ~ t , w t − u ⟩ \sum_{t=1}^{T}\left\langle\ell_{t}-\widetilde{\ell}_{t},w_{t}-u\right\rangle ,
since they will lead to terms on the order of 𝒪 ~ ​ ( ∑ t = 1 T ‖ w t ‖ 2 ) \widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|w_{t}\|^{2}}) , which could be arbitrarily large in an unbounded domain.
The main idea is of Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) is to
add an additional composite penalty φ t \varphi_{t} to the update, which introduces
an extra term ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u ) − φ t ​ ( w t ) \sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u)-\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) into the regret bound;
with this, the goal is to
choose φ t \varphi_{t} in such a
way that − ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( w t ) -\sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) is large enough to cancel with the ‖ w t ‖ \|w_{t}\| -dependent terms left over from the Martingale concentration argument, while also ensuring that ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u ) \sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u) is not too large. Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) provide a Huber-like penalty which satisfies both of these conditions (see Section ˜ D.3.1 ).

The difficulty with the above approach is that by introducing the composite penalty,
we change the OLO learner’s feedback: on round t t , the learner’s feedback becomes g ~ t = g t + ∇ φ t ​ ( w t ) \widetilde{g}_{t}=g_{t}+\nabla\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) instead of just g t ∈ ∂ ℓ t ​ ( w t ) g_{t}\in\partial\ell_{t}(w_{t}) ,
and the ∇ φ t ​ ( w t ) \nabla\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) dependence would itself
lead to ‖ w t ‖ \|w_{t}\| -dependent penalties
in the bound. This issue can be fixed
using an optimistic update by setting hints h t = ∇ φ t ​ ( w t ) h_{t}=\nabla\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) , so that
the usual ∑ t = 1 T ‖ g ~ t ‖ 2 \sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\widetilde{g}_{t}\|^{2} penalties in the final bound become ∑ t = 1 T ‖ g ~ t − h t ‖ 2 = ∑ t = 1 T ‖ g t ‖ 2 \sum_{t=1}^{T}\|\widetilde{g}_{t}-h_{t}\|^{2}=\sum_{t=1}^{T}\|g_{t}\|^{2} . Note that setting h t h_{t} this way requires solving an
implicit equation for w t = x t − y t ​ ∇ φ t ​ ( w t ) w_{t}=x_{t}-y_{t}\nabla\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) .
The full pseudocode is provided in Algorithm ˜ 2 ,
and it makes the following guarantee. 3 3 3 The theorem statement in Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) is given in terms of 𝒪 ~ ​ ( log ⁡ ( 1 / δ ) ) \widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\log\left(1/\delta\right)) ,
which hides a T T dependency inside the logarithm, as can be observed from the
parameter settings for c 1 c_{1} and c 2 c_{2} in Theorem D.1 . We highlight this T T dependence
in our re-statement to facilitate a more direct comparison to the
high-probability bounds of Zimmert & Lattimore ( 2022 ) .

Note that via Section ˜ 2 , we have that ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 ≤ 4 ​ d 2 ​ G 2 \|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|^{2}\leq 4d^{2}G^{2} almost surely, and 𝔼 ​ [ ‖ ℓ ~ t ‖ 2 | w t ] ≤ 2 ​ d ​ G \mathbb{E}\left[\|\widetilde{\ell}_{t}\|^{2}|w_{t}\right]\leq 2dG . Hence, to achieve
static regret guarantees we can immediately apply the algorithm of Zhang & Cutkosky ( 2022 ) to get the following guarantee.
The only modification we need to make is
that we should multiply the c 1 c_{1} and c 2 c_{2} in Theorem ˜ D.1 by 2, to account for the fact that we have an extra ‖ w t ‖ \|w_{t}\| -dependent concentration penalty coming from our perturbation ⟨ ℓ t , H t 1 2 ​ s t ⟩ \left\langle\ell_{t},H_{t}^{\frac{1}{2}}s_{t}\right\rangle .
See 4.2

sections.heading: D.3High-Probability Dynamic Regret Guarantees
sections.text:
In this section we derive our high-probability guarantees for dynamic regret,
culminating in the proof of Theorem ˜ 4.3 in Section ˜ D.3.3 .
We first collect
a few useful lemmas
related to the choice of composite penalty in Section ˜ D.3.1 , which will let us cancel out the ‖ w t ‖ \|w_{t}\| -dependent terms that result from the Martingale concentration argument in Section ˜ 4 In Section ˜ D.3.2 we
show how to introduce an additional
factor of ∑ t = 1 T φ t ​ ( u t ) − φ t ​ ( w t ) \sum_{t=1}^{T}\varphi_{t}(u_{t})-\varphi_{t}(w_{t}) into an algorithm’s regret guarantee
without otherwise significantly changing the algorithms original regret bound, using a careful use of optimism.
Finally, in Section ˜ D.3.3 we combine each of these pieces to
prove Theorem ˜ 4.3 .
